content.text: Node.js is a runtime environment that executes JavaScript outside the browser, directly on a computer or server. It takes the V8 engine — the same engine Chrome uses — and embeds it in a standalone program that can read files, open network connections, spawn child processes, and interact with the operating system. The result is that JavaScript, once confined to web pages, became a capable general-purpose server-side language.
Node.js is a C++ program that bundles the V8 JavaScript engine together with a set of built-in modules —

for path manipulation, and many more — and a cross-platform asynchronous I/O library called libuv. Together these components let a single JavaScript file run a web server, query a database, transform files, or orchestrate other programs.
Crucially, Node.js has no browser APIs. There is no
, and no DOM. Instead Node.js offers

(binary data), and access to the full operating system. The JavaScript language itself — variables, functions, classes, async/await, modules — is identical across environments; only the platform APIs differ.

Node.js also gave rise to npm (the Node Package Manager), which hosts over 2 million open-source packages. The combination of the runtime and this package ecosystem makes Node.js the foundation for most modern JavaScript tooling: bundlers like Webpack and Vite, testing frameworks like Jest, and server frameworks like Express all run on Node.js.

Before Node.js, using JavaScript on the server was impractical. JavaScript was a browser-only language with no standard file system or network APIs. Every web team that wanted to use JavaScript in the browser but needed a server had to maintain two completely separate codebases in two different languages — one for the front end (JavaScript) and one for the back end (PHP, Ruby, Python, Java).

Node.js solved this by making JavaScript viable on the server. Teams can now share code between the browser and the server — utility functions, data-validation logic, type definitions, rendering logic — in a single language. Developers who know JavaScript can contribute to both sides of an application. This drastically reduces the context-switching cost for full-stack development.

Node.js also proved that non-blocking, event-driven servers could handle high concurrency efficiently without the overhead of a thread per connection. An I/O-bound server that handles thousands of simultaneous connections (chat, real-time dashboards, API gateways) does not need an OS thread for each — the event loop handles them all on a single thread.
One language for front-end, back-end, CLI tools, build scripts, and tests. Shared code, shared types, shared mental model.
File reads, network calls, and database queries are async. The event loop keeps the server responsive while I/O operations are in flight — no threads to manage, no thread pools to size.
The largest package registry in the world. Authentication libraries, database clients, validation, parsing, logging, scheduling — there is almost always a well-maintained package available.
Node.js processes start in milliseconds. This makes it well-suited for serverless functions, CLI tools, and lambda-style microservices where cold start time matters.
module enables processing large files or network payloads incrementally without loading everything into memory.
Node.js supports ES modules, async/await, optional chaining, nullish coalescing, and most modern ECMAScript features without needing a transpiler for server code.

CPU-intensive work (image processing, cryptography, complex JSON parsing over huge payloads) blocks the event loop and makes the server unresponsive. Node.js is poorly suited for sustained heavy computation on the main thread.
An unhandled promise rejection or uncaught exception can crash the entire process if not managed. Robust error handling and process monitors are essential in production.

Early Node.js code relied heavily on callbacks, leading to deeply nested "callback hell." Modern Node.js mitigates this with Promises and async/await, but legacy codebases can still be difficult to follow.

Large Node.js codebases often adopt TypeScript to catch type errors at compile time. Raw JavaScript lacks the static guarantees that Java or Go provide, which can lead to runtime errors in untested code paths.
Code written for Node.js cannot use
(before Node 18). Packages that rely on browser globals fail unless polyfilled or replaced.
The V8 Engine in a New Context
Node.js embeds V8 as a library and drives it from C++ code. When Node.js starts, it initializes V8, registers its built-in modules as global objects (e.g.,

), and then compiles and runs the JavaScript entry file. V8 compiles JavaScript to machine code via JIT — the same as in the browser — and the Node.js C++ layer intercepts calls to built-in modules to execute them as OS operations.

libuv is a cross-platform C library that provides asynchronous I/O using the most efficient mechanism available on each OS (epoll on Linux, kqueue on macOS, IOCP on Windows). Node.js delegates all I/O to libuv, which runs an event loop with several phases:
— I/O callbacks deferred from the previous tick.
— retrieves new I/O events and runs their callbacks. If no events are ready, the event loop blocks here waiting.
callbacks (run after I/O, before the next timer phase).
For operations that would block the OS (file I/O, DNS lookups, zlib), libuv uses a thread pool (4 threads by default, configurable via
). These threads handle the blocking work; when complete, they notify the event loop, which runs the JavaScript callback.
). Since Node 12, it also supports ES modules (
. Modern projects prefer ES modules for their static analysis benefits and alignment with browser module behavior.

The diagram below shows how the key components of Node.js interact. V8 handles JavaScript execution; libuv manages the event loop, timer phases, and a thread pool for blocking I/O; Node.js Core APIs wrap operating system capabilities; and the OS provides the actual file system, networking, and process management.

attrs.alt: Node.js runtime architecture: V8 engine, Node.js Core APIs, libuv event loop with phases and thread pool, and OS resources (file system, networking, processes)

content.text: Notice the absence of a DOM or browser APIs on the left. The Node.js Core APIs (fs, http, path, crypto, stream) serve the same role that browser Web APIs serve in the browser — they are the platform-specific capabilities wired into the JavaScript runtime.
Node.js excels at I/O-bound workloads where many operations are happening concurrently but each one is mostly waiting for a response from a database, file system, or external service.
— receive requests, query a database, format and return JSON. Typical I/O-bound pattern.
— chat servers, live notifications, collaborative tools. WebSocket connections are lightweight on the event loop.
— all modern JavaScript build tooling (Vite, webpack, ESLint, TypeScript compiler) runs on Node.js.
— AWS Lambda, Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge Functions. Node.js cold start is fast enough for function-as-a-service workloads.
— reading a large file, transforming its contents, and writing the output. Node.js streams process data incrementally without loading it all into memory.

avoid it for CPU-intensive tasks that run for more than a few milliseconds — video encoding, scientific simulations, machine learning inference, or complex image processing. These block the event loop and prevent the server from handling other requests. Languages like Go, Rust, Python with C extensions, or Java are better choices for sustained CPU-heavy workloads. For Node.js, offload such work to a child process, a Web Worker thread, or a dedicated service.

The recommended way to install Node.js for development is through a version manager. This allows switching between versions easily, which matters because different projects may require different Node.js versions.

# macOS / Linux — install nvm, then use it to install Node.js
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.7/install.sh | bash
source ~/.bashrc          # or ~/.zshrc
nvm install 20            # install Node.js 20 (LTS)
nvm use 20
node --version            # v20.x.x
npm --version             # 10.x.x

# Windows — use nvm-windows or install the official installer from nodejs.org
A Node.js program is a plain

node app.js
node --watch app.js   # restart automatically when the file changes (Node 18+)

mkdir my-api && cd my-api
npm init -y                    # create package.json with defaults
npm install express            # install a package
node index.js                  # run the entry file
Introductory: Read and Write a File
module. The file reads and writes are performed asynchronously by libuv's thread pool; the event loop is free to handle other callbacks while waiting.

Each incoming request triggers the callback. Node.js handles multiple simultaneous connections because accepting a TCP connection is non-blocking — the event loop picks up new connections while existing ones are being processed.

A production REST API typically uses a framework like Express to handle routing, middleware, and error handling. The following shows a realistic API endpoint that reads from a database and handles errors correctly.

Never hardcode ports, hostnames, passwords, or API keys. Use
Since Node.js 15, unhandled promise rejections terminate the process. Add a global safety net:
to log and shut down gracefully.
Use a process manager in production.
Tools like PM2 or systemd restart the process if it crashes, enable clustering across CPU cores, and provide logging and health monitoring.
to declare the required Node.js version. This prevents version mismatch bugs between local development and production.
A single Node.js process uses one CPU core. On a machine with 8 cores, 7 cores would otherwise sit idle. The
module (or PM2) forks multiple worker processes, each binding to the same port, and the OS distributes incoming connections among them.

When serving or processing large files, streams prevent loading the entire file into memory. The pipeline below reads a compressed file and serves it decompressed to the client — at any point only a small chunk is in memory.
Auto-restarts server on file changes (like --watch but older)
Minimal, flexible routing and middleware for HTTP servers
High-performance alternative to Express with built-in schema validation
Clustering, auto-restart on crash, log management in production
Loads .env files into process.env for local development
Slower startup, GIL limits true threading
Smaller ecosystem; requires learning a second language
High memory usage under load; inconsistent ecosystem
Never embed credentials in source code or configuration files committed to version control. Use
for all secrets. For local development, a
) holds per-developer values; production credentials are injected by the deployment platform (Heroku config vars, AWS Secrets Manager, Kubernetes secrets).

All data arriving from HTTP requests — query parameters, JSON bodies, headers — comes from untrusted sources. Validate it before using it. Libraries like Zod, Joi, or Fastify's built-in JSON schema validation reject malformed input before it reaches business logic.
package sets security-related HTTP headers (Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, etc.) with a single line. The
package protects public endpoints from brute-force and DDoS attempts.

Think of Node.js as a restaurant with one very fast waiter (the event loop). The waiter takes orders (requests), sends them to the kitchen (OS / libuv / databases), and immediately goes to take the next order while the kitchen works. When the kitchen is done, it rings a bell (callback) and the waiter delivers the result. The waiter is never standing idle waiting for one dish — they are always moving. This model works brilliantly as long as no single order requires the waiter to personally cook for 10 minutes (CPU-bound work). For that, you hire a chef from the back kitchen (a worker thread or a child process) to handle it independently.
Node.js embeds the V8 engine in a standalone runtime with built-in modules for file I/O, networking, and OS access — enabling JavaScript to run as a full-featured server-side language.
, or browser APIs in Node.js. The Node.js Core API (fs, http, path, stream, etc.) replaces them.
libuv provides cross-platform asynchronous I/O and a multi-phase event loop that keeps the server non-blocking even under high connection counts.
Node.js excels at I/O-bound work (APIs, real-time, file streaming) and struggles with sustained CPU-bound work — offload that to worker threads or a separate service.
, async/await) and handle all promise rejections — unhandled rejections crash the process in Node.js 15+.
, validate all incoming data, and add security headers with
before deploying any Node.js server to production.

npm's 2 million+ package ecosystem and Node.js's role as the host for all major JavaScript build tools make it the foundation of the modern JavaScript development workflow — even for purely browser-based projects.
